description: A flow diagram (Fig. 11.3.1) showing how attention pooling computes a weighted average of values. It illustrates the pipeline: queries and keys feed into an attention scoring function 'a', the scores pass through softmax to produce attention weights, which then weight the values to produce the output.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual of the full attention computation pipeline — from raw scores through softmax normalization to the final weighted value aggregation — making the abstract formula tangible.
when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on attention mechanisms, before introducing specific scoring functions like dot-product or additive attention, to establish the shared computational framework.
suggested_caption: Attention pooling: scoring function + softmax yields weighted value average. (D2L.ai)

description: A code output visualization (likely a heatmap or tensor printout) showing the result of applying DotProductAttention to a toy minibatch with batch size 2, 10 keys/values of dimension 4, and valid lengths 2 and 6. Expected output shape is 2×1×4.
teaching_value: Lets students verify their understanding of scaled dot-product attention by seeing the actual numerical output, reinforcing how valid lengths mask out irrelevant keys and how the output tensor shape is determined.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the DotProductAttention implementation and before students run the code themselves, to set expectations for what correct output looks like.
suggested_caption: DotProductAttention output: 2×1×4 tensor for minibatch size 2. (D2L.ai)

description: A second code output or visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely showing attention weights or an intermediate result (e.g., softmax scores) for the same minibatch setup with valid lengths 2 and 6.
teaching_value: Provides an additional view of the scaled dot-product attention computation, helping students trace how masking and softmax interact to produce sparse or concentrated attention distributions.
when_to_show: Show alongside image 2 when walking through the DotProductAttention example step by step, to illustrate intermediate attention weight distributions.
suggested_caption: Attention weights from DotProductAttention with valid-length masking. (D2L.ai)

description: A third output visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely a heatmap of attention weights across the 10 keys for each of the 2 batch examples, showing how valid lengths 2 and 6 concentrate attention on valid positions.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the effect of valid-length masking: attention is forced to zero on padded positions, helping students understand why masking is critical for variable-length sequences.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how valid lengths prevent the model from attending to padded positions, using the contrast between the two batch examples (valid lengths 2 vs. 6) as a teaching moment.
suggested_caption: Heatmap of masked attention weights: valid lengths 2 vs. 6. (D2L.ai)

description: A fourth output visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely showing the final output tensor values or a summary plot confirming the 2×1×4 output shape after weighted value aggregation.
teaching_value: Completes the walkthrough of DotProductAttention by showing the final aggregated output, reinforcing how attention weights combine values into a single context vector per query.
when_to_show: Show as the concluding step of the DotProductAttention example to tie together scoring, masking, softmax, and aggregation into one complete forward pass.
suggested_caption: Final DotProductAttention output: weighted value aggregation result. (D2L.ai)

description: A code output or visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example with queries of shape (2,1,20), keys (2,10,2), values (2,10,4), and valid lengths (2,6). Likely shows the output tensor or attention scores for the first step of the additive attention computation.
teaching_value: Helps students see how additive attention handles queries and keys of different dimensionalities (20-dim queries vs. 2-dim keys) by projecting them into a shared hidden space, contrasting with dot-product attention.
when_to_show: Show when introducing additive attention and contrasting it with dot-product attention, emphasizing that it works when query and key dimensions differ.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention output for 20-dim queries and 2-dim keys. (D2L.ai)

description: A second output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely showing attention weights or intermediate MLP activations for the same (2,1,20)/(2,10,2)/(2,10,4) setup with valid lengths (2,6).
teaching_value: Illustrates how the tanh-activated MLP in additive attention produces attention scores, helping students connect the mathematical formula (W_q*q + W_k*k passed through tanh then w_v) to actual computed values.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the internal MLP computation of additive attention, to ground the abstract weight matrices W_q, W_k, w_v in a concrete numerical example.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention intermediate scores via tanh MLP. (D2L.ai)

description: A third output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely a heatmap of softmax attention weights showing how valid-length masking affects the distribution across 10 keys for each of the 2 batch examples.
teaching_value: Allows direct visual comparison with the DotProductAttention heatmap (image 4), showing that both methods produce similar masking behavior despite different scoring mechanisms, reinforcing the role of softmax and masking.
when_to_show: Show side-by-side with the DotProductAttention heatmap to help students compare how the two scoring functions produce different raw scores but similar masked attention patterns.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention weight heatmap with valid-length masking applied. (D2L.ai)

description: A fourth output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely showing the final 2×1×4 output tensor after weighted value aggregation, completing the additive attention forward pass demonstration.
teaching_value: Closes the additive attention example by showing the final context vectors, allowing students to compare the numerical outputs of additive vs. dot-product attention on the same value set and understand that both produce the same output shape.
when_to_show: Show as the final step of the AdditiveAttention walkthrough, then prompt students to compare this output with the DotProductAttention output to discuss similarities and differences.
suggested_caption: AdditiveAttention final output: 2×1×4 context vectors produced. (D2L.ai)

description: Architecture diagram of a sequence-to-sequence model showing an RNN encoder processing an input sequence (e.g., 'They', 'are', 'watching', '.') into a fixed-shape context vector, which initializes an RNN decoder that generates an output sequence (e.g., 'Ils', 'regardent', '.'). Shows token-by-token processing with hidden state transitions and the encoder-decoder boundary.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual of how variable-length input sequences are compressed into a fixed context vector and how that vector bootstraps the decoder, making the information bottleneck and sequential generation process tangible.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of introducing seq2seq models, before any code, to anchor the high-level data flow from source tokens to target tokens.
suggested_caption: Seq2seq RNN encoder–decoder for machine translation. (D2L.ai Fig. 10.7.1)

description: Detailed implementation diagram of the seq2seq decoder showing how the encoder's final hidden state initializes the decoder, and how the context variable is concatenated with decoder inputs at every time step before being fed into the RNN layers, with a fully connected output layer on top.
teaching_value: Clarifies two non-obvious implementation details: (1) the encoder's final hidden state is reused as the decoder's initial hidden state, and (2) the context vector is concatenated to every decoder input—not just the first—helping students understand how source information is continuously injected.
when_to_show: Show when students are about to implement or inspect the decoder class, especially when explaining why the decoder input dimension differs from the embedding dimension alone.
suggested_caption: Decoder detail: context vector concatenated at every time step. (D2L.ai)

description: Training loss curve (loss vs. epoch or iteration) for the seq2seq model on a machine translation dataset, showing the loss decreasing over training steps for one framework variant.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that the seq2seq model is learning; students can observe convergence behavior and use it as a baseline to compare against their own training runs or hyperparameter changes.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the masked cross-entropy loss and teacher forcing, as confirmation that the training procedure works and to prompt discussion of convergence speed.
suggested_caption: Training loss curve for seq2seq machine translation model. (D2L.ai)

description: Training loss curve for the seq2seq model, likely from a second framework implementation (e.g., MXNet or JAX variant), showing loss decreasing over training iterations.
teaching_value: Allows cross-framework comparison of training dynamics; students can see that the same architecture trained with different backends produces similar convergence patterns, reinforcing framework-agnostic understanding.
when_to_show: Show alongside Image 3 when discussing how results compare across implementations, or when a student is using a specific framework and wants to verify expected behavior.
suggested_caption: Training loss curve (alternate framework) for seq2seq model. (D2L.ai)

description: Training loss curve for the seq2seq model from a third framework variant, showing the characteristic decreasing loss trajectory over training.
teaching_value: Further reinforces convergence behavior across implementations; useful for discussing reproducibility and the robustness of the seq2seq training procedure.
when_to_show: Show when discussing reproducibility across frameworks or when a student asks whether training results are consistent regardless of the deep learning library used.
suggested_caption: Training loss curve (third framework variant) for seq2seq. (D2L.ai)

description: Training loss curve for the seq2seq model from a fourth framework variant (e.g., TensorFlow), showing loss decreasing over training steps.
teaching_value: Completes the cross-framework picture of seq2seq training convergence; students can compare all four curves to understand that the learning dynamics are consistent across PyTorch, MXNet, JAX, and TensorFlow.
when_to_show: Show as part of a side-by-side comparison of all training curves when emphasizing that the mathematical model is framework-independent.
suggested_caption: Training loss curve (fourth framework variant) for seq2seq. (D2L.ai)

description: Diagram illustrating the seq2seq prediction (inference) process: the encoder processes the source sequence, and the decoder generates tokens one at a time, feeding each predicted token as input to the next step, starting from a special beginning-of-sequence token and stopping at an end-of-sequence token.
teaching_value: Highlights the key difference between training (teacher forcing) and inference (autoregressive generation), showing students how the model is self-conditioned at test time and why greedy decoding works step by step.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from the training section to the prediction/inference section, to make the contrast between teacher forcing during training and autoregressive generation during inference explicit.
suggested_caption: Autoregressive seq2seq prediction: each output feeds the next step. (D2L.ai Fig. 10.7.3)

description: Diagram showing the context vector in a seq2seq encoder-decoder architecture, visualizing the context as a fixed-size vector (size 4 shown) produced by the encoder RNN and passed to the decoder RNN, with hidden states illustrated at each time step.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students what the 'context vector' is — a fixed-size numerical array — and how it serves as the sole information bridge between encoder and decoder, making the bottleneck problem intuitive.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the encoder-decoder bottleneck problem, right before explaining why attention was needed; helps students visualize what information is compressed into a single vector.
suggested_caption: Context vector (size 4) bridges encoder and decoder in seq2seq. — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram illustrating word embeddings transforming discrete words into dense vectors, and showing how an RNN processes sequential inputs by combining an input embedding vector with the previous hidden state at each time step to produce a new hidden state.
teaching_value: Gives students a visual language for two foundational concepts: (1) how words become vectors via embeddings, and (2) the recurrent mechanics of how hidden states are updated step-by-step through a sequence.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how raw text is fed into an RNN encoder; use it to ground the abstract notion of 'hidden state' before moving to attention mechanisms.
suggested_caption: Word embeddings feed into RNN steps, updating hidden states sequentially. — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram contrasting classic seq2seq (single context vector) with an attention-based model, showing the encoder passing all hidden states — one per input token — to the decoder instead of only the final hidden state.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the key architectural change that attention introduces: the decoder now has access to the full sequence of encoder hidden states, directly illustrating why attention overcomes the bottleneck limitation.
when_to_show: Show at the moment of introducing attention as a solution to the context vector bottleneck; contrast side-by-side with the context vector diagram (image 1) to highlight the architectural difference.
suggested_caption: Attention: encoder passes all hidden states to decoder, not just the last. — Jay Alammar

description: Heatmap-style visualization showing attention weights between source (French) and target (English) words during decoding, illustrating which input words the model focuses on at each output decoding step, with non-monotonic alignments visible.
teaching_value: Provides an interpretable, concrete example of attention alignment, showing students that the model learns non-trivial word correspondences (e.g., reordering between French and English) rather than simple left-to-right alignment.
when_to_show: Show after explaining how attention scores are computed, to demonstrate what the model has learned; particularly effective for illustrating that attention captures linguistic structure like word reordering.
suggested_caption: Attention heatmap shows learned French-English word alignments during decoding. — Jay Alammar

description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on other facial features — illustrating selective visual attention.
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, concrete analogy for how attention mechanisms work: focusing on high-resolution regions of interest while perceiving surroundings at lower resolution, directly motivating the mathematical formulation of attention.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of an attention lesson to build intuition before introducing any equations — ask students 'what are you looking at first and why?' to prime the concept.
suggested_caption: Visual attention: high-res focus on key regions, low-res periphery.

description: A sentence with attention weight visualizations showing how each word attends to other words in the same sentence, with varying highlight intensities indicating attention strength between word pairs.
teaching_value: Bridges the gap between visual attention and linguistic attention, showing concretely how a word's representation can depend on other words in the sentence — directly motivating self-attention in NLP models.
when_to_show: Show after the visual attention analogy and before introducing the mathematical definition of attention, to transition from images to text-based attention.
suggested_caption: Each word 'attends' to others with varying strength in a sentence.

description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing an RNN encoder processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which a decoder then uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.
teaching_value: Clearly illustrates the bottleneck problem of fixed-length context vectors in seq2seq models — the entire source sentence must be compressed into one vector — motivating why attention was invented.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitations of vanilla seq2seq models, before introducing attention — ask students 'what happens to early words when the sentence is very long?'
suggested_caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: source sentence compressed into one context vector.

description: Detailed architecture diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention mechanism in an encoder-decoder model, showing bidirectional encoder hidden states, alignment score computation, context vector as a weighted sum, and the decoder producing output tokens.
teaching_value: Shows exactly how attention resolves the bottleneck: instead of one fixed vector, the decoder dynamically computes a context vector as a weighted combination of all encoder hidden states at each decoding step, with the alignment weights visualized.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck problem; walk through each component step-by-step — encoder states, score function, softmax weights, weighted sum — before presenting the equations.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau additive attention: dynamic context from all encoder hidden states. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)

description: Heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; cell intensity shows the attention weight between each source-target word pair, revealing mostly diagonal alignment with some cross-language reordering.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that learned attention weights correspond to meaningful linguistic alignments, including non-monotonic reorderings between languages, validating the theoretical motivation for attention.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the attention weight formula to demonstrate that the model learns interpretable alignments — use the off-diagonal cells to discuss language reordering.
suggested_caption: Attention alignment matrix: French-to-English word correspondences learned automatically. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)

description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red and surrounding words are shaded in blue with varying intensity, showing which previous words the model attends to when processing the current word.
teaching_value: Demonstrates self-attention applied within a single sequence, showing that a word's representation is informed by contextually relevant earlier words — key to understanding intra-sequence dependencies.
when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention as distinct from encoder-decoder attention — highlight that both query and keys come from the same sequence.
suggested_caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to prior context (blue intensity). (Cheng et al., 2016)

description: Image captioning example showing a photo of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with attention weight overlays highlighting which image regions the model focuses on when generating each word of the caption.
teaching_value: Illustrates soft attention applied to image regions for caption generation, showing that attention weights form a soft probability distribution over spatial locations — contrasting with hard attention that selects a single patch.
when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs. hard attention — use the overlay to explain that soft attention is differentiable (weighted sum) while hard attention would select exactly one region.
suggested_caption: Soft attention over image regions for caption generation. (Xu et al., 2015)

description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams comparing Luong's global attention (left, attending over all source positions) and local attention (right, attending over a window of source positions centered on a predicted alignment point), both showing the decoder hidden state, attention layer, and context vector computation.
teaching_value: Visually contrasts two attention variants — global (soft, full sequence) vs. local (hard-soft hybrid, windowed) — helping students understand the computational trade-off between coverage and efficiency.
when_to_show: Show after covering Bahdanau attention and soft/hard distinction — use the diagrams to compare architectures side by side and discuss when local attention is preferable for long sequences.
suggested_caption: Global vs. local attention: full sequence vs. windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)

description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing the controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) producing attention weight vectors over memory rows.
teaching_value: Shows how attention mechanisms extend beyond sequence-to-sequence tasks to external memory access — the read/write heads use soft attention over memory locations, connecting NTM to the broader attention framework.
when_to_show: Show when extending attention concepts to memory-augmented networks — draw the parallel between NTM memory addressing and encoder-decoder attention weights explicitly.
suggested_caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller reads/writes memory via soft attention. (Graves et al., 2014)

description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over shift positions and a direct representation using a cosine basis.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the design choices for location-based addressing in NTMs, showing concretely how shift distributions can be parameterized differently and what each looks like visually.
when_to_show: When explaining NTM addressing mechanisms, specifically after introducing content-based addressing and interpolation gating, to illustrate how location-based shifts are represented.
suggested_caption: Two parameterizations of shift weighting distribution s_t in NTMs.

description: A detailed flow diagram showing the full addressing pipeline in a Neural Turing Machine, including content-based addressing, interpolation gating, location-based (convolutional) shift, and sharpening steps that produce the final attention weight vector w_t.
teaching_value: Provides a complete visual walkthrough of how NTM attention is computed step-by-step, allowing students to trace each transformation from key query to final attention weights and understand how the components interact.
when_to_show: After introducing each individual NTM addressing component (content, interpolation, shift, sharpening), show this diagram as a synthesis to help students see the full pipeline at once.
suggested_caption: Full NTM addressing pipeline: content, interpolation, shift, and sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)

description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence and a decoder RNN using attention over encoder hidden states to output pointers (indices) back into the input sequence rather than fixed vocabulary tokens.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how Pointer Networks differ from standard seq-2-seq models by using attention scores directly as output probabilities pointing to input positions, making the concept of variable-size output spaces concrete.
when_to_show: When introducing Pointer Networks as a solution to problems where output elements are positions in the input (e.g., sorting, TSP), to contrast with standard attention-based seq-2-seq models.
suggested_caption: Pointer Network uses attention scores as pointers to input positions. (Vinyals et al., 2015)

description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) the scaled dot-product attention computation with Q, K, V matrices, softmax, and optional masking; (right) multiple parallel attention heads whose outputs are concatenated and linearly projected.
teaching_value: Clearly illustrates both the internal mechanics of scaled dot-product attention and how multiple heads are run in parallel and combined, helping students understand why multi-head attention can capture information from different representation subspaces.
when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer architecture, introduce this diagram when explaining the core attention mechanism before discussing encoder/decoder structure.
suggested_caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads concatenated. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder block showing the stack of: input embeddings with positional encoding, multi-head self-attention layer, add & normalize (residual connection), feed-forward network, and another add & normalize step, repeated N times.
teaching_value: Shows students the exact layer composition of the Transformer encoder, including residual connections and layer normalization, helping them understand how information flows through the encoder stack.
when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer encoder specifically, after explaining multi-head attention, to show how attention is embedded within the full encoder block with normalization and residual connections.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder: self-attention, residual connections, and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of the Transformer decoder block showing masked multi-head self-attention, cross-attention over encoder output (K, V from encoder, Q from decoder), feed-forward layers, residual connections, layer normalization, and final linear+softmax output projection.
teaching_value: Illustrates how the decoder differs from the encoder by incorporating masked self-attention (to prevent attending to future tokens) and cross-attention to encoder representations, clarifying the information flow in generation.
when_to_show: After explaining the encoder, show this when teaching how the decoder generates output tokens, emphasizing the masking mechanism and the cross-attention connection to encoder states.
suggested_caption: Transformer decoder with masked self-attention and encoder cross-attention. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Complete Transformer architecture diagram showing the full encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right) side by side, with input/output embeddings, positional encodings, all attention layers, feed-forward layers, and the final output softmax.
teaching_value: Provides a unified view of the entire Transformer model, allowing students to see how encoder and decoder interact and trace the full data flow from input tokens to output probabilities.
when_to_show: After teaching encoder and decoder components separately, show this as a synthesis diagram to help students integrate their understanding of the full model.
suggested_caption: Complete Transformer architecture with encoder and decoder stacks. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Architecture diagram of the SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) model showing interleaved temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and attention layers (for pinpointing specific past information) used for meta-learning tasks.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how attention mechanisms can be combined with convolutional layers to handle both local temporal structure and long-range dependencies, and how this hybrid approach addresses the Transformer's weakness with positional ordering in RL settings.
when_to_show: When discussing limitations of pure attention models (weak positional encoding) and how hybrid architectures address them, particularly in the context of meta-learning or reinforcement learning.
suggested_caption: SNAIL interleaves causal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)

description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the receptive field of a convolutional operation (limited local region) versus self-attention (global, all-to-all connections across the entire image), illustrating the difference in spatial coverage.
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive visual explanation of why self-attention is more powerful than convolution for capturing long-range spatial dependencies, directly motivating the use of self-attention in GANs.
when_to_show: When introducing SAGAN or explaining the motivation for adding self-attention to convolutional architectures, to make the receptive field limitation of convolutions concrete and intuitive.
suggested_caption: Convolution has local receptive field; self-attention covers global spatial context.

description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing how image feature maps x are projected via 1x1 convolutions into query (f), key (g), and value (h) branches, combined via softmax to produce an attention map, then used to compute the self-attention output added back to the input.
teaching_value: Concretely shows how the Transformer's Q/K/V attention framework is adapted to 2D image feature maps using 1x1 convolutions, helping students transfer their understanding of NLP attention to the vision domain.
when_to_show: When teaching how attention mechanisms generalize beyond NLP to computer vision, specifically when explaining SAGAN or non-local neural networks.
suggested_caption: SAGAN self-attention: image features projected to Q, K, V via 1x1 convolutions. (Zhang et al., 2018)

description: A grid of 128×128 generated images from the Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) model, showing diverse object classes such as dogs, birds, and other animals with fine-grained details and coherent global structure.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical output quality of SAGAN, showing students how self-attention mechanisms in GANs enable the model to capture long-range spatial dependencies and generate globally consistent, high-resolution images — something local convolutions alone struggle with.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention formulation (attention map α_ij, 1x1 conv filters W_f/W_g/W_h) to give students a concrete visual payoff — illustrating why capturing non-local relationships matters for realistic image generation.
suggested_caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showing globally coherent structure. (Zhang et al., 2018)

description: High-level diagram of the Transformer architecture showing the encoding component (stack of encoders) and decoding component (stack of decoders) with connections between them, resembling an 'opened up' black box view.
teaching_value: Gives students their first internal view of the Transformer, establishing the two major components (encoder stack and decoder stack) and their relationship before diving into details.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the Transformer as a black box, to transition students from the external view to the internal component structure.
suggested_caption: Transformer internals: encoder stack and decoder stack with connections. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the Transformer as a black box for machine translation, with an input sentence in one language entering and a translated output sentence exiting, labeled with encoder and decoder components.
teaching_value: Anchors the abstract architecture in a concrete use case (machine translation), helping students understand the input-output behavior before exploring internal mechanics.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a Transformer lesson to ground students in the practical application and establish the high-level input/output framing.
suggested_caption: Transformer as a black box: translates input sentence to output. (Alammar)

description: Diagram illustrating the stacked encoder-decoder architecture with six encoders stacked on the left and six decoders stacked on the right, with arrows showing information flow between the stacks.
teaching_value: Makes the concept of stacking explicit and visual, helping students understand that depth comes from repeating identical structural units and that encoders feed into decoders.
when_to_show: Show when explaining why the Transformer uses multiple layers and how the encoder output connects to the decoder stack.
suggested_caption: Six stacked encoders feed into six stacked decoders in the Transformer. (Alammar)

description: Diagram of a single Transformer encoder block showing two sub-layers: a Self-Attention layer at the bottom and a Feed-Forward Neural Network layer on top, with arrows indicating data flow upward.
teaching_value: Clearly decomposes the encoder into its two sub-layers, allowing students to understand the internal structure before learning how each sub-layer works in detail.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the internal structure of a single encoder, before explaining self-attention or feed-forward mechanics in detail.
suggested_caption: Single encoder block: self-attention layer feeds into feed-forward network. (Alammar)

description: Diagram of a single Transformer decoder block showing three sub-layers: Self-Attention, Encoder-Decoder Attention (cross-attention), and a Feed-Forward Neural Network, with arrows showing data flow.
teaching_value: Highlights the key structural difference between encoder and decoder — the additional cross-attention sub-layer — helping students understand how the decoder attends to encoder outputs.
when_to_show: Show when contrasting the decoder structure with the encoder, specifically to explain the role of the encoder-decoder attention (cross-attention) layer.
suggested_caption: Decoder block adds cross-attention layer to attend to encoder output. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing word embedding: input words (e.g., 'Thinking', 'Machines') being converted into fixed-size vectors (size 512) represented as colored bars, fed into the bottom encoder.
teaching_value: Visualizes the embedding step concretely, showing that discrete words become continuous vectors of a specific dimensionality before entering the Transformer, bridging NLP preprocessing to the model.
when_to_show: Show when introducing how raw text is prepared as input to the Transformer, before explaining how vectors flow through the encoder layers.
suggested_caption: Input words converted to 512-dimensional embedding vectors before encoding. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing how word embedding vectors flow through the two sub-layers (self-attention and feed-forward) of the encoder, with each word's vector following its own path through the encoder.
teaching_value: Introduces the concept of parallel processing paths per token and shows how tensors flow through the encoder, making the data flow concrete and helping students visualize the per-position processing.
when_to_show: Show after introducing embeddings, when explaining how vectors travel through the encoder and why positions can be processed in parallel in the feed-forward layer.
suggested_caption: Each word vector flows independently through self-attention then feed-forward layers. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing a shorter example sentence being encoded, illustrating how each word's representation flows through the encoder sub-layers with arrows indicating the sequential and parallel processing paths.
teaching_value: Reinforces the encoding process with a simpler example, making it easier to trace individual token paths and understand the distinction between self-attention dependencies and feed-forward parallelism.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from the high-level encoder overview to a detailed walkthrough of what happens inside each sub-layer, using a concrete short sentence.
suggested_caption: Shorter sentence example tracing token paths through encoder sub-layers. (Alammar)

description: Visualization of self-attention showing the word 'it' in a sentence attending to 'animal' with highlighted attention weights, demonstrating how self-attention links pronouns to their referents.
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, concrete example of what self-attention computes — showing that the model learns to associate related words across a sentence — making the abstract mechanism tangible.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the self-attention explanation to motivate WHY self-attention is useful before explaining HOW it is computed mathematically.
suggested_caption: Self-attention links 'it' to 'animal', capturing long-range word dependencies. (Alammar)

description: Diagram illustrating the creation of Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors from input word embeddings by multiplying with learned weight matrices WQ, WK, WV, showing the dimensionality reduction from 512 to 64.
teaching_value: Explains the first concrete computational step of self-attention — the Q/K/V projection — and clarifies the role of each vector type and the dimensionality choice, grounding the abstract QKV abstraction in matrix operations.
when_to_show: Show when beginning the mathematical walkthrough of self-attention, specifically to explain what Q, K, and V are and how they are derived from input embeddings.
suggested_caption: Query, Key, Value vectors created by multiplying embeddings with learned weight matrices. (Alammar)

description: Step-by-step diagram showing how self-attention scores are computed for the word 'Thinking' by taking dot products of its query vector (q1) with the key vectors (k1, k2) of each word in the sequence, producing raw attention scores.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the dot-product scoring mechanism, showing students exactly which vectors interact and why — making the abstract formula q·k tangible with labeled arrows and named vectors.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing Q, K, V vectors, when explaining step 2 of self-attention — before normalization or softmax are introduced.
suggested_caption: Step 2: Raw attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Alammar)

description: Diagram extending the scoring process to show steps 3 and 4: dividing raw scores by √64 (scaling), then applying softmax to produce normalized attention weights that sum to 1, alongside step 5 of multiplying value vectors by these weights.
teaching_value: Explains why scaling is necessary (gradient stability) and how softmax converts raw scores into a probability distribution, helping students understand the full pipeline from raw score to weighted values.
when_to_show: Show after the dot-product scoring diagram, when walking through steps 3–5 of self-attention to explain normalization and the role of softmax.
suggested_caption: Steps 3–5: Scale by √d_k, apply softmax, weight value vectors. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing step 6 of self-attention: summing the softmax-weighted value vectors to produce the final output vector z1 for the first word position, completing the self-attention calculation.
teaching_value: Closes the loop on the full self-attention computation, showing students how a single output vector is assembled as a weighted combination of all value vectors — making the aggregation step concrete.
when_to_show: Show as the concluding step of the word-level self-attention walkthrough, just before transitioning to the matrix form of the calculation.
suggested_caption: Step 6: Sum weighted value vectors to produce self-attention output z1. (Alammar)

description: Matrix calculation diagram showing how input embeddings packed into matrix X are multiplied by trained weight matrices W^Q, W^K, and W^V to simultaneously produce the Query, Key, and Value matrices for all positions.
teaching_value: Bridges the word-level intuition to the efficient matrix implementation, helping students understand how the same computation is parallelized across all tokens using matrix multiplication.
when_to_show: Show after completing the word-level self-attention walkthrough, when introducing the matrix form to explain how transformers are implemented efficiently in practice.
suggested_caption: Matrix form: X multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V yields Q, K, V matrices. (Alammar)

description: Single condensed formula diagram showing the full self-attention computation as Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k)V, collapsing steps 2–6 into one matrix expression.
teaching_value: Presents the canonical self-attention formula in visual form, allowing students to connect each matrix operation back to the individual steps they already learned, reinforcing the complete algorithm.
when_to_show: Show after the step-by-step matrix diagrams, as a synthesis moment — ideal for students ready to see the compact mathematical expression of the full mechanism.
suggested_caption: Full self-attention formula: softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V in matrix form. (Alammar)

description: Diagram illustrating multi-headed attention by showing the same Q, K, V calculation performed eight times in parallel with different weight matrices (W^Q_0…W^Q_7, etc.), producing eight separate Z matrices.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how multi-head attention multiplies representational capacity by running independent attention computations, helping students grasp why multiple heads enable richer contextual understanding.
when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-headed attention, after students understand single-head self-attention — use to motivate why multiple heads are beneficial.
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention: eight parallel Q/K/V projections with distinct weight matrices. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the eight Z matrices (Z0–Z7) produced by eight attention heads, illustrating the challenge that the feed-forward layer expects a single matrix rather than eight separate ones.
teaching_value: Sets up the problem that motivates the concatenation + projection step, helping students understand why an additional weight matrix W^O is needed to reconcile multi-head outputs.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the multi-head Q/K/V diagram, to pose the 'how do we combine eight matrices?' problem before revealing the W^O solution.
suggested_caption: Eight attention heads produce eight Z matrices requiring consolidation. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing how the eight Z matrices from multi-head attention are concatenated and then multiplied by a learned weight matrix W^O to produce a single output matrix passed to the feed-forward layer.
teaching_value: Explains the final projection step of multi-head attention, showing students how learned linear combination of all heads' outputs creates a unified representation — completing the multi-head mechanism.
when_to_show: Show as the resolution to the 'eight matrices problem', directly after presenting the Z0–Z7 outputs — completes the multi-head attention explanation.
suggested_caption: Concat eight Z matrices, multiply by W^O to produce single attention output. (Alammar)

description: Comprehensive recap diagram showing all components of multi-headed self-attention in one visual: input embeddings, eight sets of W^Q/W^K/W^V matrices, eight Z outputs, concatenation, W^O projection, and final output.
teaching_value: Provides a unified reference diagram that consolidates all multi-head attention steps, helping students see the full pipeline at once and review how each component connects — ideal for consolidation.
when_to_show: Show as a summary after explaining all individual steps of multi-head attention — use as a reference diagram for review or when students need to see the big picture.
suggested_caption: Complete multi-head self-attention pipeline from embeddings to output. (Alammar)

description: Attention visualization showing which tokens the word 'it' attends to across different attention heads when encoding the sentence 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired', with colored lines indicating attention weights.
teaching_value: Provides an interpretable, real example of what attention heads learn — showing students that different heads capture different linguistic relationships (e.g., coreference), making abstract attention weights meaningful.
when_to_show: Show after explaining multi-head attention mechanics, as a motivating real-world example — especially effective when discussing what attention heads learn or why multiple heads are useful.
suggested_caption: Attention heads reveal 'it' refers to 'animal' in coreference resolution. (Alammar)

description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to all other tokens, creating a visually complex web of attention weights across multiple heads.
teaching_value: Demonstrates why multi-head attention is hard to interpret when all heads are shown together, motivating the need to examine heads individually and understand that different heads capture different linguistic relationships.
when_to_show: After explaining individual attention heads; use to show the student why multi-head attention is powerful but complex, and to prompt discussion about interpretability challenges in transformers.
suggested_caption: All attention heads for 'it' — multi-head attention is hard to interpret. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing input word embeddings (e.g., for 'You', 'are', 'amazing') each being added element-wise to a corresponding positional encoding vector of the same dimensionality (4 in this simplified example), producing position-aware input vectors.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how positional encoding is injected into the model by vector addition to embeddings, making abstract the idea that position information is encoded as a fixed vector added before any processing begins.
when_to_show: When introducing positional encoding; show this before the heatmap visualizations to give students an intuitive, low-dimensional example of how position vectors are combined with embeddings.
suggested_caption: Positional encoding vectors added to word embeddings (dim=4 example). (Alammar)

description: Small color-coded heatmap grid showing positional encoding values for a few word positions across a 4-dimensional embedding space, with values ranging from -1 to 1 represented by color intensity.
teaching_value: Provides a minimal, readable example of the positional encoding pattern before scaling up, helping students see the sinusoidal structure at a glance without being overwhelmed by 512 dimensions.
when_to_show: Immediately after introducing the concept of positional encoding vectors; use as a stepping stone before showing the full 512-dimension heatmap to build intuition incrementally.
suggested_caption: Positional encoding pattern for 4-dimensional embeddings, color-coded. (Alammar)

description: Large color-coded heatmap with rows representing sequence positions (up to ~20 words) and 512 columns representing embedding dimensions, showing the sinusoidal positional encoding pattern with alternating bands of color that shift across dimensions.
teaching_value: Reveals the full sinusoidal structure of positional encodings at model scale, showing students how different dimensions encode position at different frequencies and why this scheme generalizes to unseen sequence lengths.
when_to_show: After the 4-dim example; use to show the real-scale pattern and discuss how the frequency structure allows the model to generalize to longer sequences than seen during training.
suggested_caption: Full 512-dim positional encoding heatmap; each row is one word position. (Alammar)

description: Heatmap from the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper showing positional encodings with an interleaved sinusoidal pattern (alternating sine/cosine per dimension pair), visually distinct from the Tensor2Tensor concatenated version shown earlier.
teaching_value: Highlights the difference between the paper's original interleaved positional encoding and the Tensor2Tensor implementation, teaching students to critically compare implementations and understand that multiple valid approaches exist.
when_to_show: When discussing the July 2020 update note; use to compare with the previous heatmap and explain that the paper interleaves sine/cosine signals rather than concatenating them.
suggested_caption: Original paper's interleaved sinusoidal positional encoding pattern. (Vaswani et al. / Alammar)

description: Diagram of a single encoder layer showing the self-attention sub-layer with a residual (skip) connection that adds the original input back to the sub-layer output, followed by a Layer Normalization step, then the feed-forward sub-layer with its own residual connection and Layer Norm.
teaching_value: Clearly shows the Add & Norm pattern that wraps every sub-layer in the transformer encoder, helping students understand how residual connections and layer normalization stabilize training and preserve gradient flow.
when_to_show: When introducing the residuals section; use to show the structural pattern before presenting the stacked encoder/decoder diagram, so students understand the building block first.
suggested_caption: Residual connection and layer normalization around each encoder sub-layer. (Alammar)

description: Architecture diagram of a Transformer with 2 stacked encoders and 2 stacked decoders, each encoder and decoder block showing residual connections and layer normalization around self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers, with arrows indicating data flow from encoder stack to decoder stack.
teaching_value: Shows how the Add & Norm pattern scales across a full stacked Transformer, helping students see the complete encoder-decoder architecture with residuals at every level and understand how encoder outputs feed into decoders.
when_to_show: After explaining individual encoder residuals; use to zoom out and show the full 2-layer Transformer, bridging the sub-layer detail to the complete model architecture.
suggested_caption: Two-layer Transformer with residual connections and layer norm throughout. (Alammar)

description: Detailed vector-level diagram of a single encoder's self-attention sub-layer, showing input vectors flowing into the self-attention block, the residual addition of the original input vectors to the attention output, and the resulting vectors entering layer normalization.
teaching_value: Makes the residual connection concrete at the vector level, showing students exactly which vectors are added together and how the dimensions must match, reinforcing understanding of why residuals require consistent dimensionality.
when_to_show: When a student needs a more granular view of the Add & Norm operation; use alongside the architectural diagram to connect the high-level pattern to actual vector arithmetic.
suggested_caption: Vector-level view of residual addition and layer norm after self-attention. (Alammar)

description: Animated GIF showing the first decoding step of a Transformer: the encoder stack processes the full input sequence, producing K and V vectors, while the decoder stack begins generating the first output token using the encoder's output and a start token as input.
teaching_value: Animates the transition from encoding to decoding, helping students visualize the autoregressive generation process and understand how encoder outputs (K, V) are passed to the decoder's encoder-decoder attention layer.
when_to_show: When introducing the decoder side; use this first animation to show the initial decoding step before showing the subsequent steps, establishing the overall generation loop.
suggested_caption: First decoding step: encoder outputs K,V vectors used by decoder. (Alammar)

description: Animated GIF showing multiple decoding steps of a Transformer, with the decoder generating output tokens one at a time, each new token fed back as input for the next step, illustrating the autoregressive loop until an end-of-sequence token is produced.
teaching_value: Illustrates the full autoregressive decoding loop, showing students how previously generated tokens become inputs for subsequent steps and how the decoder attends to both its own outputs and the encoder's K/V vectors at each step.
when_to_show: After the first decoding step animation; use to show the complete generation process and discuss masked self-attention (future positions set to -inf) and how the decoder builds output sequentially.
suggested_caption: Autoregressive decoding loop: each output token feeds the next step. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the final stages of the Transformer decoder: a logits vector of 10,000 cells (one per vocabulary word) produced by the Linear layer, followed by a Softmax layer that converts scores to probabilities, with the highest-probability cell selected as the output word.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how raw decoder outputs (logits) are transformed into a probability distribution over the vocabulary and how a single output word is selected, bridging abstract math to the actual word-generation mechanism.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how the Transformer produces its final word prediction — specifically when transitioning from internal representations to human-readable output tokens.
suggested_caption: Linear layer produces logits; Softmax converts them to word probabilities. (Alammar)

description: A one-hot encoding vector example for a small vocabulary, showing how the word 'am' is represented as a vector with a single 1 at the position corresponding to 'am' and 0s elsewhere.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract concept of one-hot encoding tangible by showing exactly how a discrete vocabulary word maps to a fixed-length binary vector, which is foundational for understanding both input embeddings and output target labels.
when_to_show: Show when introducing how words are represented as vectors during training, especially before discussing loss functions that compare model output distributions to target one-hot vectors.
suggested_caption: One-hot encoding represents 'am' as a single-1 binary vector. (Alammar)

description: A one-hot encoded vector over a six-word vocabulary ('a', 'am', 'i', 'thanks', 'student', '<eos>'), visually showing which position is active for a specific word, used to illustrate target labels in training.
teaching_value: Grounds the concept of one-hot encoding in a minimal, concrete vocabulary example, helping students see exactly how a target word becomes a training signal vector that the model's output distribution is compared against.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how training targets are formatted — particularly when setting up the loss function discussion and the 'merci' → 'thanks' translation example.
suggested_caption: Six-word vocabulary with one-hot target vector for training. (Alammar)

description: Side-by-side comparison of the model's output probability distribution (logits/softmax output across vocabulary) versus the one-hot target label for the word 'thanks', illustrating how loss is computed by comparing the two distributions.
teaching_value: Directly visualizes the core training signal: the gap between what the model predicts and what the correct answer is, making cross-entropy loss intuitive before the formal definition is introduced.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the loss function — use it to motivate why we need a metric to measure the difference between predicted and target distributions.
suggested_caption: Model output vs. target distribution — the basis of cross-entropy loss. (Alammar)

description: A sequence of probability distributions the model should ideally output at each decoding step for translating 'je suis étudiant' → 'i am a student', showing one distribution per output token with the correct word having near-zero probability in an untrained model.
teaching_value: Illustrates the sequential nature of decoder output and what the training objective looks like across multiple time steps, helping students understand that the model must learn to produce correct distributions at every position in the output sequence.
when_to_show: Show when explaining multi-step training targets — after the single-word loss example, to scale up to realistic sentence-level training scenarios.
suggested_caption: Target probability distributions at each decoding step during training. (Alammar)

description: Probability distributions produced by a well-trained Transformer model at each decoding step for 'i am a student', showing high probability mass concentrated on the correct output word at each position, contrasting with the untrained model's distributions.
teaching_value: Shows what successful training looks like — the model's output distributions become sharply peaked at correct words — and sets up the discussion of greedy decoding vs. beam search by showing how a word is selected from each distribution.
when_to_show: Show after presenting the untrained/target distributions to contrast before-and-after training, and to introduce decoding strategies like greedy decoding and beam search.
suggested_caption: Trained model outputs near-perfect probability distributions per decoding step. (Alammar)

description: A schematic of the query–key–value attention mechanism: a query q is compared against multiple keys k_i to produce attention weights (via a compatibility function and softmax), which are then used to compute a weighted sum of the corresponding values v_i (attention pooling) to produce an output.
teaching_value: Helps students connect the math (softmax-normalized compatibility scores) to the computation graph: scoring q vs. k_i, turning scores into a probability distribution, and using it to pool values into a single context/output vector.
when_to_show: Show when introducing attention for the first time or when a learner confuses keys vs. values; use it to walk through the three steps: score, normalize, pool.
suggested_caption: Query–key scoring and softmax weights pool values into an output (D2L).

description: Sequence-to-sequence RNN encoder–decoder diagram where an encoder reads source tokens x1…xT into hidden states and compresses them into a single fixed-length context/state vector, which is then provided to the decoder to generate target tokens y1…yT'.
teaching_value: Helps students see the classic bottleneck: all source information must fit into one fixed-dimensional vector, motivating attention as a remedy.
when_to_show: Show right after introducing vanilla seq2seq for translation, before explaining why attention is needed.
suggested_caption: Vanilla RNN seq2seq uses a single context vector bottleneck (Dive into Deep Learning).

description: Detailed seq2seq-with-attention schematic: encoder produces hidden states at all time steps; at each decoder step, the previous decoder hidden state acts as a query over encoder states (keys/values) via additive (Bahdanau) attention to produce a context vector, which is combined with the decoder input to update the decoder state and generate the next token.
teaching_value: Makes the alignment idea concrete by showing how per-step context is computed from all encoder states, clarifying query/key/value roles and the recurrent use of attention during decoding.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from fixed context to attention; use it to walk through one decoding time step end-to-end.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau attention computes a per-step context from encoder states (Dive into Deep Learning).

description: Diagram of RNN encoder–decoder with attention-like skip connections: multiple encoder hidden states feed into each decoder step via weighted connections, producing a context vector as a weighted sum rather than relying only on the final encoder state.
teaching_value: Helps learners see why a single fixed context vector is a bottleneck and how per-step weighted combinations of encoder states let the decoder focus on different source tokens at different output times.
when_to_show: Show right after explaining the limitation of using only the final encoder state, before introducing formal attention scoring/softmax; use it to motivate time-varying weights per decoder step.
suggested_caption: Weighted skip connections form a context vector from encoder states (source: pantelis.github.io).

description: Step-specific seq2seq attention visualization for a French→English example at a later decoder time (described as time step 6): attention weights (softmax over alignment scores) concentrate on the source object token, yielding a context vector used to produce the current target word.
teaching_value: Demonstrates that attention distributions change across decoding steps and that the context vector is recomputed each step to emphasize the most relevant source word(s) for the current target prediction.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how attention shifts during decoding; ask the student to identify which source token receives the highest weight and relate it to the target word generated at that step.
suggested_caption: Attention at decoder step 6 focuses on the source object (source: pantelis.github.io).

description: Step-specific seq2seq attention visualization for the same French→English example at an early decoder time (described as time step 1): attention weights concentrate on the source pronoun, forming the context vector used for the first target prediction.
teaching_value: Provides a contrasting example to later steps, reinforcing that attention is dynamic and that early target words often align to different source positions than later target words.
when_to_show: Show alongside the later-step figure to compare distributions; prompt the student to explain why the pronoun is most relevant at the first decoding step.
suggested_caption: Attention at decoder step 1 emphasizes the source pronoun (source: pantelis.github.io).

description: A schematic of human vs machine translation: a source sentence x1..xm in one language mapped to a target sentence y1..yn in another, emphasizing variable lengths and the goal of maximizing conditional probability p(y|x).
teaching_value: Connects the intuitive translation task to the formal learning objective (argmax over p(y|x)), clarifying inputs/outputs and why sequence lengths can differ.
when_to_show: Use at the start of a seq2seq lesson to define x and y, and when introducing the formal objective y* = argmax_y p(y|x).
suggested_caption: Machine translation as maximizing p(y|x) over variable-length sequences (Voita NLP Course).

description: Encoder-decoder block diagram: an encoder consumes the source tokens and produces a representation; a decoder generates target tokens conditioned on that representation, illustrating the standard seq2seq framework.
teaching_value: Gives students the core mental model shared by RNN-based seq2seq and Transformers: separate encoding of the source and autoregressive decoding of the target.
when_to_show: Show right before comparing model families (RNN seq2seq vs attention/Transformer) to anchor all variants in the same encoder-decoder template.
suggested_caption: Encoder-decoder: encode source, then decode target autoregressively (Voita NLP Course).

description: Side-by-side comparison of a standard language model versus a conditional language model: LM predicts next token from previous target tokens only, while CLM additionally conditions predictions on a source input x.
teaching_value: Helps learners reuse LM intuition for seq2seq by isolating the single change: conditioning on x, which motivates training and inference procedures identical to LMs but with extra context.
when_to_show: Use when students already know LMs and you want to explain seq2seq training as the same next-token prediction objective plus source conditioning.
suggested_caption: Seq2seq as conditional language modeling: predict y_t given y_<t and x (Voita NLP Course).

description: A detailed CLM pipeline diagram: encoder processes source x; decoder produces hidden states for each timestep; a linear layer plus softmax outputs a vocabulary distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x) at each step.
teaching_value: Makes the computation graph concrete (hidden state → linear → softmax), clarifying where probabilities come from and how per-step predictions form the full sequence probability.
when_to_show: Show when deriving the training loss or explaining how a decoder turns hidden states into token probabilities over the vocabulary.
suggested_caption: Decoder hidden state → linear layer → softmax gives p(y_t|y_<t,x) (Voita NLP Course).

description: The simplest seq2seq model with two RNNs (often LSTMs): an encoder RNN reads the source sequence; its final state initializes the decoder RNN, which then generates the target sequence token by token.
teaching_value: Illustrates the classic bottleneck approach (single final encoder state) and sets up why attention is needed when long inputs must be remembered during decoding.
when_to_show: Use before introducing attention to highlight the limitation of compressing the whole source into one vector/state.
suggested_caption: Basic RNN seq2seq: encoder final state initializes decoder (Voita NLP Course).

description: A visualization of sentence representations from the last encoder state: multiple example sentences plotted so that semantically similar sentences cluster together despite different word order/structure.
teaching_value: Provides empirical intuition that encoder states capture meaning, not just surface form, and motivates using learned representations for downstream decoding.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the encoder state to build intuition about what it encodes, or when discussing why seq2seq can generalize across paraphrases.
suggested_caption: Last encoder states cluster sentences with similar meaning (Voita NLP Course).

description: Single-timestep training illustration: the model outputs a probability distribution over the vocabulary; the gold target is a one-hot vector for y_t; cross-entropy encourages high probability on the correct token.
teaching_value: Links the abstract cross-entropy formula to the concrete next-token prediction step used in seq2seq training, making the loss intuitive and actionable.
when_to_show: Use when introducing the seq2seq objective or when students confuse sequence-level loss with per-token loss; walk through one timestep then sum over t.
suggested_caption: Per-step cross-entropy: push probability mass onto the correct token y_t (Voita NLP Course).

description: An equation-style graphic showing inference as an argmax over sequences: y' = argmax_y p(y|x, θ), highlighting that exact search over |V|^n sequences is infeasible.
teaching_value: Frames decoding as a search problem and motivates approximate methods (greedy, beam search) by making the combinatorial explosion explicit.
when_to_show: Show right before introducing greedy decoding and beam search to justify why we need heuristics instead of exact maximization.
suggested_caption: Inference is argmax over sequences; exact search is intractable (Voita NLP Course).

description: A small example demonstrating greedy decoding failure: choosing the most probable token at each step leads to a worse overall sequence than an alternative with lower early probability but higher total probability.
teaching_value: Concretely shows why local decisions don’t optimize global sequence probability, motivating beam search as a practical improvement.
when_to_show: Use when students ask why not just take argmax at each timestep; step through the example and compare total sequence scores.
suggested_caption: Greedy decoding can miss the best sequence; local argmax isn’t global (Voita NLP Course).

description: Illustration of the seq2seq bottleneck: an encoder must compress an entire source sentence into a single fixed-size vector passed to the decoder, highlighting information loss for long/complex inputs.
teaching_value: Makes the limitation of fixed encoder representations concrete, motivating why attention is needed to avoid forcing all meaning into one vector.
when_to_show: Show right after introducing vanilla seq2seq to explain why translation quality degrades on long sentences.
suggested_caption: Fixed-vector bottleneck in vanilla seq2seq (source: lena-voita.github.io).

description: High-level attention overview: at each decoder time step, the model computes attention over encoder states to focus on different source tokens and form a step-specific context vector.
teaching_value: Provides the big-picture workflow of attention (scores → weights → weighted sum → context), helping students map later formulas to the overall pipeline.
when_to_show: Show when first defining attention to anchor subsequent details (score function, softmax, weighted sum).
suggested_caption: Attention lets the decoder focus on different source parts each step (source: lena-voita.github.io).

description: Detailed attention computation scheme: encoder produces a sequence of hidden states; decoder state queries them to compute attention scores, applies softmax to get weights, forms a weighted sum context vector, and uses it to produce the next output.
teaching_value: Connects each computational block to its role in end-to-end training, clarifying what is learned (scores/weights) and what is passed forward (context).
when_to_show: Show when walking through one decoder step mathematically or in pseudocode, to align symbols with blocks.
suggested_caption: Attention step: scores → softmax weights → context vector (source: lena-voita.github.io).

description: End-to-end differentiable attention pipeline in a seq2seq decoder step: decoder state (query) is compared to all encoder hidden states (keys/values) to produce attention scores, softmax attention weights, a weighted-sum context vector, and then the next-token prediction; arrows indicate gradients flow through score function, softmax, and weighted sum.
teaching_value: Helps students see the full computation graph for attention and why it can be trained end-to-end: attention weights are learned implicitly via backprop rather than hard alignment rules.
when_to_show: Show right after introducing seq2seq to explain how the decoder consults all encoder states at each time step and to motivate that attention weights are learned via gradients.
suggested_caption: Differentiable attention computation at one decoding step (Voita NLP course).

description: Close-up schematic of the attention-score computation block, highlighting the inputs used to compute a score for each source position (e.g., decoder state/query with an encoder state/key) before softmax produces attention weights.
teaching_value: Focuses attention on the key design choice: the score function. It clarifies what information is available when computing alignment-like scores and sets up different scoring variants.
when_to_show: Show when students ask 'what exactly is scored in attention?' or before presenting dot-product/additive score formulas.
suggested_caption: What goes into an attention score: decoder state vs. encoder states (Voita NLP course).

description: Comparison table/strip of common attention score functions used in early seq2seq models, typically including dot product, general (bilinear with a matrix), and additive/MLP (Bahdanau) forms, showing their formulas and required parameters.
teaching_value: Lets students compare scoring options by complexity and parameterization, and understand how different compatibility functions change what the model can learn without changing the overall attention pipeline.
when_to_show: Show when choosing an attention variant for an implementation or when contrasting additive vs dot-product behavior and efficiency.
suggested_caption: Popular attention score functions: dot, general, additive (Voita NLP course).

description: Bahdanau (additive) attention model diagram: encoder produces hidden states; at each decoder step an MLP combines decoder state with each encoder state to compute scores, softmax yields weights, context vector is formed, and context is combined with decoder state to produce output and next state.
teaching_value: Shows the canonical additive-attention architecture and where the extra neural layer sits, helping students map equations to modules and understand how context influences decoding.
when_to_show: Show when introducing historical NMT attention (2014–2015) or when deriving the additive attention equations from the generic pipeline.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau additive attention in encoder–decoder NMT (Voita NLP course).

description: Luong attention model diagram: encoder hidden states are scored against the current decoder state using dot/general/concat scoring; attention weights produce a context vector; context is combined with decoder state (often via a linear layer + tanh) to form an attentional hidden state used for prediction.
teaching_value: Helps students distinguish Luong’s formulation from Bahdanau’s (where attention is applied relative to decoder state timing and how the combined 'attentional' state is formed), clarifying common terminology used in NMT literature.
when_to_show: Show when comparing Bahdanau vs Luong or when explaining why dot-product-style scoring is computationally attractive.
suggested_caption: Luong (multiplicative) attention variant for NMT (Voita NLP course).

description: Example attention heatmaps/matrices from NMT showing source words on one axis and generated target words on the other; darker cells indicate higher attention weights, illustrating near-diagonal soft alignments with occasional reordering.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract idea of attention concrete by visualizing learned soft word alignment, helping students interpret attention weights and understand how the decoder focuses on different source tokens over time.
when_to_show: Show after students learn how weights are computed, to practice reading attention maps and diagnosing whether a model attends sensibly.
suggested_caption: Attention weights form soft source–target alignments (Voita NLP course).

description: Comparison table contrasting RNNs, CNNs, and self-attention/Transformer along modeling dimensions such as maximum path length between tokens, parallelizability, and computational complexity per layer, emphasizing why Transformers train faster and capture long-range dependencies.
teaching_value: Provides a structured rationale for the Transformer shift: students can connect architectural choices to practical tradeoffs (parallel training, dependency length, compute), not just performance claims.
when_to_show: Show when motivating Transformers after seq2seq attention, especially to answer 'why not just use RNNs?' with concrete tradeoffs.
suggested_caption: Modeling tradeoffs: RNN/CNN vs self-attention (Voita NLP course).

description: Animated schematic of the original Transformer encoder–decoder: stacked encoder layers with self-attention and feed-forward blocks, stacked decoder layers with masked self-attention, encoder–decoder attention, and output generation; shows parallel token processing and attention connections.
teaching_value: Gives learners an intuitive, high-level mental model of Transformer data flow (self-attention within encoder/decoder and cross-attention between them) before diving into equations.
when_to_show: Show as an overview right before detailing self-attention and masking, or when students need to locate where cross-attention happens in the decoder.
suggested_caption: Original Transformer encoder–decoder overview (Google AI blog).

description: Word-based intuitive diagram of Transformer encoder/decoder behavior: tokens in the encoder interact simultaneously via self-attention; decoder generates tokens step-by-step while attending to encoder outputs, emphasizing 'all-at-once' contextualization in the encoder.
teaching_value: Bridges the gap between the animation and formal blocks by restating the process in plain language, helping students internalize parallel contextual encoding vs autoregressive decoding.
when_to_show: Show after the Transformer animation to confirm understanding, or when introducing why self-attention helps disambiguate word meaning using full-sentence context.
suggested_caption: Transformer in words: parallel encoder, autoregressive decoder (Voita NLP course).

description: Illustration comparing RNN encoding vs Transformer self-attention on a sentence containing the ambiguous word 'bank' (river bank): RNN must process sequentially to accumulate context, while Transformer allows immediate token-to-token interactions to resolve meaning earlier via self-attention layers.
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive example of long-range dependency and ambiguity resolution, showing why self-attention’s global context and short path lengths can outperform sequential recurrence for understanding.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the practical advantage of self-attention for meaning disambiguation and why Transformers handle long sentences better than RNNs.
suggested_caption: RNN vs Transformer: resolving “bank” using global context (Voita NLP course).

description: Side-by-side schematic contrasting decoder self-attention (tokens in the target prefix attending to each other) with encoder-decoder (cross) attention (decoder tokens attending to encoder hidden states). Shows information flow arrows from previous target tokens and from source representations into the current decoder layer.
teaching_value: Clarifies that a Transformer decoder layer has two distinct attention operations: causal self-attention for target history and cross-attention to condition on the source, explaining why both are required for translation.
when_to_show: When a learner confuses self-attention with cross-attention, or when introducing the decoder layer sequence: self-attend to prefix, then attend to encoder outputs.
suggested_caption: Decoder self-attention vs cross-attention in a Transformer decoder layer (Voita).

description: Illustration of query, key, and value vectors derived from each token embedding/hidden state, with arrows indicating how a token’s query compares to other tokens’ keys to produce attention weights, which then weight the corresponding values to form the output representation.
teaching_value: Builds intuition for Q/K/V roles by mapping them to ‘what I’m looking for’ (query), ‘what I offer’ (key), and ‘the information to pass’ (value), making the attention computation less opaque.
when_to_show: Right before presenting the attention equations, or when students ask why attention needs three projections instead of one.
suggested_caption: Q, K, V roles in self-attention: match queries to keys, mix values (Voita).

description: Compact formula diagram for scaled dot-product attention showing Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k) V, with labels indicating where scores, normalization, and value mixing occur.
teaching_value: Provides the exact computation pipeline (scores → softmax weights → weighted sum), helping students connect the intuitive Q/K/V story to the standard Transformer math.
when_to_show: When transitioning from intuition to implementation, or when deriving tensor shapes for batched attention.
suggested_caption: Scaled dot-product attention: softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V (Voita).
