description: Diagram showing multi-head attention setup where Q, K, and V are linearly projected into multiple heads (separate subspaces), attention is computed independently per head, and head outputs are concatenated and projected back.
teaching_value: Explains how multiple heads let the model attend to different relations simultaneously while keeping per-head dimensionality smaller for efficiency.
when_to_show: After students understand single-head attention and ask what ‘heads’ are or why they help capture different dependencies.
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention: split Q/K/V into heads, attend independently, then combine (Voita).

description: Full Transformer architecture diagram with stacked encoder layers on the left and stacked decoder layers on the right, including self-attention, encoder-decoder attention in the decoder, feed-forward blocks, residual connections with Add & Norm, and the final linear + softmax output.
teaching_value: Gives a global mental model of how encoder and decoder stacks interact and where each component (self-attention, cross-attention, FFN, residuals, layer norm) fits in the end-to-end translation pipeline.
when_to_show: When summarizing the model after covering components, or when a learner needs to locate where masking/cross-attention/FFN occurs in the full network.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with attention, FFN, and Add & Norm blocks (Voita).

description: Block diagram of the Transformer position-wise feed-forward network: a linear layer expanding dimensionality, ReLU activation, then a second linear layer projecting back to model dimension.
teaching_value: Highlights that FFN is applied independently to each token position (no mixing across time), complementing attention by adding nonlinearity and feature transformation.
when_to_show: When explaining what happens after attention in each layer, or when comparing attention (mix tokens) vs FFN (process per token).
suggested_caption: Transformer FFN: Linear → ReLU → Linear, applied per token position (Voita).

description: Illustration of a residual (skip) connection around a sublayer, showing the sublayer input added to its output before passing onward (often into normalization).
teaching_value: Makes the purpose and mechanics of residuals concrete, supporting understanding of why deep Transformer stacks train stably (improved gradient flow).
when_to_show: When students ask what the ‘Add’ in ‘Add & Norm’ means, or when discussing training stability in deep Transformers.
suggested_caption: Residual connection: add sublayer input to its output before the next step (Voita).

description: Diagram explaining layer normalization within the Transformer’s Add & Norm block, indicating normalization of each token’s feature vector (across hidden dimensions) to stabilize activations.
teaching_value: Helps learners distinguish layer norm from batch norm and understand why normalization is applied per example/token representation to improve convergence stability.
when_to_show: When covering ‘Norm’ in Add & Norm, or when learners confuse normalization types and axes (batch vs layer).
suggested_caption: Layer normalization in Transformer: normalize each token’s hidden features (Voita).

description: Visualization of positional encoding/positional embeddings being added to token embeddings to form the Transformer input, emphasizing that order information is injected by summation.
teaching_value: Directly addresses the key limitation (no recurrence/convolution) and shows the practical fix: combine token identity with position information before the encoder/decoder layers.
when_to_show: When introducing why Transformers need positional information, or before presenting sinusoidal positional encoding formulas.
suggested_caption: Input representation = token embedding + positional encoding to inject order (Voita).

description: Example illustrating word vs subword tokenization (Byte Pair Encoding), showing how a word can be split into frequent subword units and how sequences differ under word-level and subword-level segmentation.
teaching_value: Demonstrates why subword segmentation reduces out-of-vocabulary issues and improves handling of rare words by composing them from known pieces.
when_to_show: When explaining preprocessing for NMT/Transformers, especially if learners ask how models handle rare or unseen words.
suggested_caption: BPE subword segmentation: represent rare words as sequences of frequent subwords (Voita).

description: Animated walkthrough of BPE training: starting from character-level word segmentations, counting frequent adjacent symbol pairs within words, repeatedly merging the most frequent pair, updating the merge table and token vocabulary, and showing resulting subword tokens with continuation marker (e.g., @@).
teaching_value: Makes the iterative nature of BPE concrete: students can see how frequency-driven merges create subword units, why merges are constrained to word boundaries, and how the learned merge table/vocabulary emerge from data statistics.
when_to_show: Show when introducing how BPE learns merge rules from a corpus, before discussing how the same rules are applied at inference time.
suggested_caption: BPE training builds merge rules and a subword vocabulary (Voita, NLP Course).

description: Animated example of applying a learned BPE merge table to segment new text at inference: starting from characters, applying merges in learned priority order, producing subword tokens, and marking non-final subwords with @@ (distinguishing mat vs mat@@).
teaching_value: Clarifies the difference between training (learning merges) and inference (deterministic application of merges), and helps students understand how @@ encodes ‘token continues’ to reconstruct original words.
when_to_show: Show right after presenting the learned merge table, when students ask how to tokenize unseen words or how @@ is used in practice.
suggested_caption: Applying learned BPE merges to segment text at inference (Voita, NLP Course).

description: Schematic of the standard probing setup: take frozen model representations as features, train a lightweight classifier (probe) to predict a linguistic property/label, and evaluate what information is encoded in the representations.
teaching_value: Provides a mental model for probing: what is frozen vs trained, what the inputs/outputs are, and why probe performance is used as evidence about representational content.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from model-specific interpretability (e.g., attention heads) to model-agnostic analysis of representations using probes.
suggested_caption: Standard probing: predict labels from frozen representations (Voita, NLP Course).

description: Plot(s) of probing results for part-of-speech tagging: probe accuracy (or similar metric) versus encoder layer (embeddings, layer 1, layer 2), comparing how POS information changes across layers in an NMT encoder.
teaching_value: Helps students see that linguistic information is distributed across layers and can peak at particular depths, motivating layer-wise analysis rather than treating ‘the encoder’ as a single representation.
when_to_show: Show when explaining what probes reveal about which layers encode POS/morphology, and when discussing why intermediate layers can be most informative.
suggested_caption: POS probing across encoder layers in NMT (Voita, NLP Course).

description: Comparison chart showing the effect of target language on source-side encoder representations: POS probing performance across layers for the same source language (e.g., Arabic) trained with different target languages, illustrating that target morphology influences what the encoder learns.
teaching_value: Demonstrates that representation learning depends on the training objective and target language; students learn that ‘what the encoder encodes’ is shaped by downstream demands, not just the source language.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how changing the target language alters encoder linguistic knowledge, especially in lessons on morphology and inductive biases in NMT.
suggested_caption: Target language changes what the encoder encodes (Voita, NLP Course).

description: Diagram illustrating a basic LSTM seq2seq model without attention on long sequences: encoder compresses the whole source into a single final state/context vector, decoder generates the target from this bottleneck, highlighting difficulty with long-range dependencies.
teaching_value: Makes the information bottleneck visible, helping students understand why vanilla seq2seq struggles on long inputs and motivating attention mechanisms as a remedy.
when_to_show: Show when introducing limitations of early seq2seq models and before presenting attention as a solution.
suggested_caption: Vanilla LSTM seq2seq bottleneck on long inputs (Voita, NLP Course).

description: Before/after illustration of reversing source token order in seq2seq: original source-to-target alignment versus reversed source sequence, emphasizing shorter temporal paths between early target tokens and the most relevant source tokens.
teaching_value: Helps students grasp why reversing the source can ease optimization in LSTMs by creating more short-term connections, improving gradient flow and early decoding accuracy.
when_to_show: Show when explaining historical tricks used before attention, or when students ask why reversing inputs improved early seq2seq results.
suggested_caption: Reversing source tokens shortens dependencies for decoding (Voita, NLP Course).

description: Illustration of subword segmentation with BPE (and/or alternative segmentations) for the same word, showing how words can be split into subword units and marked with @@ to indicate continuation.
teaching_value: Builds intuition for why subword tokenization helps with rare words and morphology by sharing subword pieces across related word forms.
when_to_show: Show when introducing subword tokenization benefits (rare words, morphology) and before discussing stochastic segmentation methods like BPE-dropout.
suggested_caption: Subword segmentation example with BPE-style @@ markers (Voita, NLP Course).

description: Nearest-neighbor comparison in embedding space: contrasts embeddings learned with deterministic BPE versus stochastic segmentation (e.g., BPE-dropout), showing that stochastic segmentation yields neighbors sharing meaningful subword parts more often than standard BPE.
teaching_value: Connects tokenization choices to representation quality: students see evidence that stochastic segmentation can produce embeddings that better capture morphological similarity.
when_to_show: Show after introducing BPE-dropout to justify why it helps, especially during discussions of embedding geometry and morphological generalization.
suggested_caption: Stochastic segmentation yields more morphologically consistent neighbors (Voita, NLP Course).

description: Algorithm diagram for BPE-dropout: start from a learned BPE merge table, randomly drop a fraction of merge rules, then apply the remaining merges so that different valid segmentations are produced across passes.
teaching_value: Explains the mechanism behind subword regularization in a simple, actionable way—students can implement it by sampling merges and understand how it increases segmentation diversity during training.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to implement BPE-dropout or when contrasting deterministic vs stochastic tokenization during model training.
suggested_caption: BPE-dropout: randomly drop merges to sample segmentations (Voita, NLP Course).

description: High-level Transformer architecture diagram showing an encoder stack on the left and a decoder stack on the right, connected via attention; includes token embeddings/positional encodings at inputs and output generation at the decoder side.
teaching_value: Orients learners to where self-attention sits in the full Transformer, clarifying encoder vs decoder roles and why attention replaces recurrence for sequence modeling.
when_to_show: Use at the start to give a roadmap before deriving Q/K/V and attention math; revisit when introducing cross-attention.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder–decoder overview with attention blocks (Raschka).

description: Sentence-level illustration motivating attention: a sequence of words where understanding/translation depends on relating non-adjacent tokens rather than processing strictly left-to-right.
teaching_value: Helps students grasp the core motivation for attention: selecting relevant context across the whole sequence to resolve meaning and dependencies.
when_to_show: Show before any equations to motivate why token-to-token interactions matter, especially for translation or coreference examples.
suggested_caption: Why attention helps: words depend on distant context (Raschka).

alt: Self attention from scratch attention matrices
description: Diagram of the three projection matrices used in attention: Wq, Wk, and Wv multiplying the input embedding matrix X to produce query (Q), key (K), and value (V) matrices, with shapes annotated (e.g., Wq,Wk ∈ R^{d_k×d}, Wv ∈ R^{d_v×d}).
teaching_value: Makes tensor shapes explicit, reducing common confusion about how Q/K/V are computed from embeddings and how dimensionalities d, d_k, and d_v relate.
when_to_show: Show right after introducing embeddings and before computing dot products; use it when students get stuck on dimension mismatches in code.
suggested_caption: Q, K, V are linear projections of embeddings with defined shapes (Raschka).

description: Illustration focusing on a single token position acting as the query (the second input element), showing how its embedding is projected to q(2) and compared against keys from all positions.
teaching_value: Clarifies the per-token viewpoint: one token queries the entire sequence, which helps students connect the algorithm to implementation (looping over positions vs batched matrix ops).
when_to_show: Use when transitioning from defining Q/K/V to computing attention for one position; helpful before generalizing to full-matrix attention.
suggested_caption: Single-position query example for self-attention (Raschka).

description: Matrix-style visualization of unnormalized attention weights ω_{i,j} computed as dot products between a query vector q(i) and each key vector k(j), producing a row of scores for one query across all tokens.
teaching_value: Shows exactly where attention scores come from (similarity via dot product) and how they form an attention-weight matrix prior to softmax normalization.
when_to_show: Show when introducing ω = QK^T (or its per-row equivalent) and before applying scaling/softmax; use to explain why each query attends to all keys.
suggested_caption: Unnormalized attention weights from query–key dot products (Raschka).

alt: Self attention from scratch attention scores
description: Diagram showing scaling of ω by 1/sqrt(d_k) followed by softmax to produce normalized attention scores α, typically illustrated as a probability distribution over tokens for each query.
teaching_value: Connects numerical stability and training behavior to the math: scaling controls dot-product variance, and softmax turns scores into interpretable weights that sum to 1.
when_to_show: Use when students ask why the sqrt(d_k) term exists or when demonstrating how raw scores become probabilities; pair with a small numeric example.
suggested_caption: Scale by 1/√d_k then softmax to get attention weights (Raschka).

alt: Self attention from scratch context vector
description: Visualization of computing the context vector z(i) as a weighted sum of value vectors v(j) using the normalized attention weights α_{i,j}, yielding one output vector per query position.
teaching_value: Makes the output of attention concrete: attention doesn’t pick a single token, it blends information from all values according to learned relevance weights.
when_to_show: Show after softmax when explaining how attention produces new representations; use to bridge into the matrix form Z = AV.
suggested_caption: Context vector as attention-weighted sum of values (Raschka).

description: Summary diagram listing the tensor dimensions for a single attention head, including X, Wq/Wk/Wv, Q/K/V, ω (or scores), α (softmax weights), and the resulting context/output vectors, with example sizes (e.g., d_q=d_k=24, d_v=28).
teaching_value: Provides a compact reference for shape-checking and debugging implementations, reinforcing how each operation transforms dimensions through the attention pipeline.
when_to_show: Use as a recap after the full derivation or during coding exercises when learners encounter shape errors; keep visible as a reference sheet.
suggested_caption: Single-head attention tensor shapes summary (Raschka).

alt: Self attention from scratch cross attention
description: Diagram contrasting self-attention vs cross-attention: queries come from one sequence (e.g., decoder states) while keys/values come from a different sequence (e.g., encoder outputs), illustrating how the decoder attends to encoder representations.
teaching_value: Helps students distinguish the data flow difference between self- and cross-attention, which is essential for understanding encoder–decoder Transformers and seq2seq generation.
when_to_show: Show when moving from self-attention to encoder–decoder models, or when explaining how translation/summarization decoders use encoder context.
suggested_caption: Cross-attention: decoder queries attend to encoder keys/values (Raschka).

alt: Self attention from scratch cross attention summary
description: A schematic of cross-attention showing two sequences: one providing queries (x1) and the other providing keys and values (x2). It highlights that the sequences may have different lengths but must share the same embedding dimension, and notes that setting x1 = x2 reduces cross-attention to self-attention. It also reflects the common encoder–decoder setup where queries come from the decoder and keys/values from the encoder.
teaching_value: Helps learners distinguish cross-attention from self-attention by visually separating the query source from the key/value source, reinforcing the constraints (matching embedding size, variable sequence lengths) and the encoder–decoder intuition used in translation models.
concepts: sequence length vs embedding dimension constraints
when_to_show: Show right after introducing Q, K, V to explain how attention changes when Q comes from a different sequence than K/V, or when a student is confused about why encoder-decoder transformers need a separate attention block.
suggested_caption: Cross-attention: queries from x1 attend to keys/values from x2 (Raschka).

description: An application-oriented diagram linking cross-attention to Stable Diffusion: text prompt embeddings condition image generation by attending between text features and image (U-Net/latent) features, illustrating cross-attention between two modalities (text and image latents).
teaching_value: Connects the abstract cross-attention mechanism to a well-known real-world system, helping students generalize cross-attention beyond translation to multimodal conditioning (text-to-image), and clarifying that the two sequences can represent different modalities with different lengths.
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks 'where is cross-attention used in practice?' or when transitioning from encoder–decoder NLP examples to multimodal models like text-to-image generation.
suggested_caption: Cross-attention in Stable Diffusion: text prompts condition image features (Raschka).
